parentQuestion: Striking the 'funny bone' is actually stimulation of (or injury to) the ___.

options: A small bone in the elbow
The ulnar nerve passing behind the elbow
scaffolds.explain: The 'FUNNY BONE' is actually the ULNAR NERVE passing behind your elbow. It's called 'funny' because it creates a strange tingling sensation when bumped, not because it's humorous.

wrongExplains.0: It's not a bone at all — it's a nerve. The name is misleading.
wrongExplains.2: While the elbow is a joint, the funny bone refers to a nerve, not the joint itself.
wrongExplains.3: Laughter isn't involved. The 'funny' refers to the strange tingling sensation.

scaffolds.q: What does the ulnar nerve do?
options: Provides sensation and motor control to part of the hand
scaffolds.explain: The ULNAR NERVE provides SENSATION to the little finger and half the ring finger, plus MOTOR control to many small hand muscles. It's essential for grip strength.

wrongExplains.0: The ulnar nerve is in the arm, not the leg. Leg nerves come from the lumbar/sacral plexus.
wrongExplains.2: Blood pumping is done by the heart. Nerves carry electrical signals.
wrongExplains.3: Breathing is controlled by the phrenic nerve. The ulnar nerve serves the hand.

scaffolds.q: Why does hitting your funny bone cause tingling in your fingers?
options: Because bones vibrate into the hand
Because the ulnar nerve is compressed against the bone
scaffolds.explain: At the elbow, the ULNAR NERVE passes through the CUBITAL TUNNEL with minimal protection. When struck, it's COMPRESSED against the bone, sending signals that feel like electric tingling.

wrongExplains.0: Bones don't vibrate to cause this sensation. It's nerve compression.
wrongExplains.2: Blood flow isn't the issue. Direct nerve stimulation causes the tingling.
wrongExplains.3: The tingling is from nerve stimulation, not muscle spasms.

scaffolds.q: Which fingers feel tingling when you hit your funny bone?
scaffolds.explain: The RING FINGER and LITTLE FINGER! This is the ULNAR NERVE'S sensory territory. The thumb and index finger are supplied by the median nerve instead.

wrongExplains.0: Thumb and index finger are supplied by the median nerve, not the ulnar nerve.
wrongExplains.2: Each nerve has its own territory. The ulnar nerve only serves the ulnar side of the hand.
wrongExplains.3: The middle finger is mainly median nerve territory. Ulnar is ring and little finger.

scaffolds.q: Where is the ulnar nerve most vulnerable to injury?
scaffolds.explain: The ulnar nerve is most vulnerable at the CUBITAL TUNNEL behind the MEDIAL ELBOW. Here it lies superficially with minimal soft tissue protection — your 'funny bone' spot.

wrongExplains.0: The shoulder has other nerves but isn't where the ulnar nerve is most exposed.
wrongExplains.2: The wrist is another compression site (Guyon's canal), but the elbow is more vulnerable.
wrongExplains.3: The neck is where nerve roots exit, but the elbow is the classic vulnerability point.

scaffolds.q: Why is the ulnar nerve named 'ulnar'?
options: After the doctor who discovered it
Because it runs along the ulna bone in the forearm
Because it's shaped like the letter U
scaffolds.explain: The ULNAR NERVE is named after the ULNA — the forearm bone on your pinky side. The nerve runs along this bone from elbow to hand.

wrongExplains.0: It's named after anatomy, not a person. Ulna is the pinky-side forearm bone.
wrongExplains.2: 'Ulnar' refers to the ulna bone, not 'ultimate.'
wrongExplains.3: The shape isn't U-like. It's named for the ulna bone it travels alongside.

scaffolds.q: How many main nerves supply the hand?
options: Three main nerves: Median, ulnar, and radial
scaffolds.explain: THREE main nerves supply the hand: MEDIAN (thumb/index/middle), ULNAR (ring/little finger), and RADIAL (back of hand). Each has its own territory.

wrongExplains.0: Multiple nerves are needed. One nerve couldn't provide all the fine motor control.
wrongExplains.2: Three nerves divide the territory, not one per finger.
wrongExplains.3: The hand absolutely has nerve supply — that's how you feel and move your fingers.

scaffolds.q: What happens if the ulnar nerve is severely damaged?
options: Weakness in grip and loss of sensation in the little finger
scaffolds.explain: Severe ulnar nerve damage causes 'CLAW HAND' — weakness in grip (especially pinch strength) and LOSS OF SENSATION in the ring and little fingers. Fine motor control is impaired.

wrongExplains.0: The thumb is mainly median nerve. Ulnar damage affects the little finger side.
wrongExplains.2: Only the ulnar nerve territory is affected. Other nerves still work.
wrongExplains.3: Hearing uses the cochlear nerve. The ulnar nerve is in the arm.

scaffolds.q: Striking the 'funny bone' stimulates which nerve?
scaffolds.explain: The ULNAR NERVE! It passes through the cubital tunnel at the medial elbow, unprotected against the bone. The tingling in your ring and little fingers is classic ulnar nerve sensation.

wrongExplains.0: The median nerve is deeper and protected. It doesn't cause the 'funny bone' sensation.
wrongExplains.1: The radial nerve is on the lateral (thumb) side of the arm, not at the funny bone.
wrongExplains.3: The sciatic nerve is in the leg. The funny bone is in the arm.
